exemplar.title:
How does the use of propaganda in media and speech 
influence the manipulation of truths and influence the psyche when in a 
totalitarian regime, as reflected in George Orwell's 1984?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does the use of propaganda in media and speech 
influence the manipulation of truths and influence the psyche when in a 
totalitarian regime, as reflected in George Orwell's 1984?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: The title and introduction clearly communicate the investigationâs focus on propaganda, truth manipulation, and psychology in 1984.
Section headings and signposts maintain the central purpose throughout, and the methodology section is clearly marked and logically structured.
Analysis sections are well signposted, dividing discussion into coherent subsections.
weaknesses: The research question combines multiple elements (media, speech, truth, psyche), making the scope too broad for razor-sharp focus.
Methodology lacks justification for the chosen frameworks and does not explain selection criteria for sources.
Occasional verbosity and minor historical digressions dilute the core novel-specific analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research question focus and methodology) align with the 3â4 band, while topic communication aligns with the 5â6 band. Overall performance sits solidly in the middle of the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Demonstrates sound knowledge of Orwellâs text, accurately summarising Newspeak, doublethink and Party slogans.
Correct and recurring use of key terminology such as âpropaganda model,â âsurveillance,â and âthought police.â
Effective summarisation of the Propaganda Modelâs five filters and vivid literary analysis (e.g., the numerical torture example).
weaknesses: Some in-text citations lack precise page numbers and full references.
Occasional typographical errors (e.g., âqulaqâ for âGulagâ) and informal wording reduce precision.
Integration of secondary sources is sometimes superficial, indicating partial rather than fully coherent command of the literature.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands demonstrate clear understanding and mostly accurate application of terminology and concepts, placing performance firmly in the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects this level of competence.

strengths: The bibliography shows a fair variety of scholarly and theoretical sources that are largely relevant to the research question.
Analysis effectively links key quotations from 1984 to the Propaganda Model, demonstrating good support for claims.
Argument maintains coherence, with generally well-supported conclusions about text manipulation and theoretical implications.
weaknesses: A few sources (e.g., the Malcolm X quotation) are only loosely connected to the novelâs context and need tighter linking.
Evaluation of alternative interpretations or the limitations of the Marxist and psychological lenses is brief and underdeveloped.
Some analytical assertions (e.g., linking Winstonâs conformity to the flak filter) remain speculative without further textual or theoretical support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality and analysis strands align with the 7â9 band, while discussion and evaluation aligns with the 4â6 band. Overall performance sits at the lower end of the mid band, warranting a holistic score of 6 out of 12.

strengths: The report follows a clear, appropriate structure with all mandatory elements (title page, word count, headings, reference list).
Logical progression and a comprehensive table of contents aid navigation.
Section headings and formatting generally support readability.
weaknesses: In-text citations are inconsistently formatted and often lack page numbers.
LaTeX/Markdown artefacts remain in places, and reference list entries are sometimes duplicated or misformatted.
Minor layout issues (e.g., inconsistent spacing) distract slightly from overall presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets the top band (score of 4), but layout and formatting show recurring minor lapses (band 1â2). Averaging these strands yields a score of 3 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A methodological outline is provided: the analysis is divided into two propaganda types and employs the Propaganda Model and Marxist lens.  A reasonable range of secondary sources is listed and there is evidence of selective quotation from both novel and theory.  Nevertheless, the description of how each framework will be applied remains general, and the source base is almost entirely desk research without explanation of selection criteria.  This corresponds to a âmostly completeâ methodology.

Formatting conventions (word count, headings, reference list) are present, and most in-text citations are given.  Nevertheless, page numbers are absent in the submission extract, some LaTeX/Markdown artefacts remain, and reference formatting is inconsistent.  These minor lapses lower the score within the 3-4 band to 3.

The bibliography contains a fair variety of scholarly, historical and theoretical sources that are largely relevant to the RQ.  Most references cited in the text connect directly to propaganda or totalitarianism, though a few (e.g., Malcolm X quotation) are tangential.  Overall the research is âgoodâ and mostly pertinent, meeting mid-band expectations.

The title, opening paragraph and section headings make it immediately clear that the investigation concerns propaganda, truth-manipulation and psychology in Orwellâs 1984.  The student maintains this focus consistently, signalling purpose in the introduction and returning to it in the conclusion.  Occasional verbosity and minor digressions into real-world history do not obscure the central purpose, so the quality aligns with the upper band descriptors.

A clear argumentative line is present, but evaluation of alternative readings or limitations of sources is brief.  For instance, the Marxist lens is asserted rather than weighed against other interpretations, and psychological impact is discussed narratively rather than critically tested.  This constitutes âgood argument development with partially critical evaluation,â meriting the lower end of the 4-6 range.

Analytical passages link quotations from 1984 to theoretical frameworks, explaining how telescreens, Newspeak and Party slogans manipulate citizens.  Claims are generally supported by textual evidence; however, some assertions about real-world parallels are assumed rather than demonstrated.  The depth is therefore âgoodâ but not consistently rigorous, aligning with the middle of the 4-6 band.

The essay follows the required structure: title page with word count, clear section headings, introduction, body, conclusion, and reference list.  Logical progression is easy to follow and all mandatory elements appear, so it meets the top band for Presentation-Structure.

The student demonstrates sound knowledge of Orwellâs text, accurately summarising key concepts such as Newspeak, doublethink and Party slogans.  Secondary material from Chomsky, Marx and Arendt is introduced and linked to points, though integration is occasionally superficial.  Some citations are incomplete, suggesting competent but not excellent command of the literature, so the work fits the 3-4 descriptor.

Key subject termsâpropaganda model, doublethink, euphemism, ideological controlâare used correctly and recur throughout the argument.  Minor lapses (e.g., âqulaqâ for âGulagâ) and occasional informal wording show the terminology is âmostly accurateâ rather than consistently precise, justifying placement in band 3-4.

The research question is explicitly stated and directly tied to 1984; however, it combines several elements (âmedia,â âspeech,â âmanipulation of truths,â and âpsycheâ) which widens the scope and at times forces the essay to treat points only briefly.  While the discussion usually relates back to the question, the dual emphasis on both âtruthâ and âpsycheâ prevents truly sharp focus.  This places the work solidly in the 3-4 band rather than the fully focused 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: Explanation of the Inner/Outer Party and Proles hierarchy is clear and well integrated.
The Marxist lens discussion repeats earlier points. Consider condensing to avoid redundancy.
Use of Winstonâs statistics quote is compelling evidence and well linked to the argument.
The title clearly indicates the investigationâs focus on propaganda, truth, and psychology in 1984, setting a precise scope.
The research question combines media, speech, truth manipulation, and psyche, making the scope too broad. Consider narrowing to one or two elements for sharper focus.
Word count is provided and within limit, meeting the formatting requirement.
The table of contents provides a clear structure with all major sections labeled, aiding navigation.
No page numbers appear in the table of contents or headers. Adding consistent pagination improves usability.
The historical context paragraph is engaging but drifts from the novelâs specifics. Tighten this to directly introduce Orwellâs 1984.
Terminology such as âtotalitarian,â âsurveillance,â and âthought policeâ is used accurately, demonstrating sound subject understanding.
The paragraph on propaganda (Edelman, 1971) introduces the concept but lacks a precise citation style and page reference for the reader.
The methodology heading is clear and indicates the start of a structured methods section.
The methodology outlines two frameworks but does not explain why these were chosen over others. Justify selection criteria for sources and models.
The Propaganda Modelâs five filters are summarized clearly, demonstrating understanding of key concepts.
The Analysis section is well signposted, dividing the discussion into two clear subsections.
The Malcolm X quotation is powerful but only loosely connected to 1984âs context; explicitly link it back to the novelâs media control mechanisms.
Application of the Propaganda Model to telescreens shows good integration of theory and text evidence.
This sentence is overly verbose and could be streamlined for clarity. Break into shorter phrases.
The discussion of sourcing filter is informative but lacks critique of alternate interpretations; deepen critical analysis.
The section on speech and language is well introduced, outlining key definitions.
Citation for Newspeak examples lacks page numbers and source clarity. Add precise references.
Literary analysis of Orwellâs slogans effectively uses juxtaposition and oxymoron to explain doublethink.
The numerical torture example illustrates doublethink vividly; excellent use of LaTeX for clarity: $$2+2=4$$ vs. $$2+2=5$$.
Linking Winstonâs conformity to the flak filter is insightful but speculative. Support with additional textual or theoretical evidence.
The term âqulaqsâ appears as a typo for âGulag.â Correct spelling and provide source.
The conclusion restates analysis points but feels repetitive. Condense and explicitly reflect on the research question.
References contain inconsistent formatting and missing DOIs. Apply a single citation style consistently.
Machiavelli appears twice in the bibliography. Merge entries to avoid redundancy.
In-text quotes lack page numbers (e.g., Winstonâs statistics). Include precise page references for each citation.
